title: Natural experiments prove phytoplankton carbon removal works
excerpt: Dozens of natural events give us a clear picture of what happens when you add minerals to nutrient-deprived oceans.
content_html:
Adding nutrients to the ocean so tiny ocean plants remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere is an idea that people aren’t used to. Unfamiliar ideas make us uncomfortable. That gut level “I’m not so sure about this” is natural enough. It comes out as an inchoate sense that such a thing must be risky, that we’ve never done it before and so couldn’t possibly know what the consequences would be.

It’s not true, though. We have quite a precise understanding of what happens when you add nutrient-rich minerals to nutrient-poor waters. Dozens of natural experiments have settled the question. Because nature puts minerals into the ocean all the time, all around the world, through many different mechanisms. And it’s been doing so for millions of years.

Volcanoes do it. Wildfires do it. Even educated whales do it. Dust storms and icebergs do it too. And because satellites have been measuring the ocean’s color with minute accuracy for over twenty years, and research ships have been chasing these events with sensors and sample bottles, we have a large and growing record to show what happens when nutrient-starved water suddenly gets fed. It’s one of the best-documented cause-and-effect relationships in ocean science.

Remember, most of the open ocean is a desert. The top layer of water gets plenty of sunlight, but as you get farther from land the tiny drifting plants that form the base of the marine food web — phytoplankton — run short of one or two key ingredients, usually iron, sometimes nitrogen or phosphorus. Give them what they’re missing and they multiply, sucking carbon out of the air in the process. Take the nutrient source away, and within weeks the effect fades. The pattern has been observed over and over, in different waters, driven by different processes, sort of everywhere.

One case of particular interest to me comes from the subtropical South Pacific. It had long been hypothesized, but never proven, that shallow undersea vents near Tonga might be leaking iron-rich fluid close enough to the surface to reach sunlit water and feed nitrogen-fixing bacteria.

Then, in 2019, a research cruise went and found it.

A single string of undersea vents feeds a patch of ocean the size of Germany with enough iron to fuel a bloom of nitrogen-fixing bacteria that grew two to eight times faster than those in the surrounding water, and pulled two to three times more carbon down into the deep ocean. That work was published in Science in 2023. Follow-up cruises mapped exactly how sharply the iron levels spiked near the vents and faded away with distance, tracked the plankton response cell by cell, and even built a nitrogen budget showing that in some seasons, nearly all of the region’s biological productivity runs on vent iron.

The Tonga-Kermadec range isn’t unique, either. Similar iron-fed blooms have now been traced to volcanic ridges south of Africa, near Antarctica, and scattered across the Pacific seafloor — some fed by iron plumes that drift over a thousand kilometers before they’re used up. One recent study even linked the strength of a Southern Ocean bloom, year by year, to how many small earthquakes had recently shaken the nearby volcanic ridge: more quakes appear to be related to more iron release, which leads to more phytoplankton growth.

But in fact, undersea vents are a little niche; the best-known natural fertilizer delivery system is dust in the wind. Every year, wind lifts millions of tons of fine soil off the Sahara and carry it out over the Atlantic; the same thing happens with dust off the Gobi desert blowing over the North Pacific, dust off Patagonia settling onto the Southern Ocean, and dust off drought-stricken southern Africa reaching all the way to Madagascar. Scientists have been measuring these sorts of events for decades: wherever iron-rich dust lands on iron-starved water, phytoplankton growth follows, often within one to two weeks.

Maybe the clearest evidence comes from robotic ocean floats that happened to be sitting in the North Pacific when a Gobi dust cloud rolled over them in 2001. The floats measured the living carbon in the water nearly doubling within two weeks. A dust storm over the Arabian Sea in 2012 was followed by a nearly fivefold jump in chlorophyll. A 2024 study using over a decade of ocean-robot data concluded that nutrients from dust currently sustain roughly a third of all the plant growth happening across the entire Southern Ocean each year — and that during the last ice age, when the world was far dustier, that figure was closer to two-thirds. Deserts, in other words, have been fertilizing oceans on a hemispheric scale for as long as there have been deserts.

Volcanic ash shows these events in sharp detail, because the nutrient additions are sudden, violent, and easy to date them precisely. In August 2008, an Aleutian volcano called Kasatochi erupted and dropped ash across the Gulf of Alaska. Within days, satellites picked up one of the largest phytoplankton blooms ever recorded in that part of the Pacific. When Iceland’s Eyjafjallajökull volcano — the unpronounceable one that grounded European air traffic back in 2010 — dusted the North Atlantic with ash, research ships sailing under the plume measured a spike in dissolved iron and ran shipboard experiments showing the ash was actively feeding the local plankton. The same thing happened after Hawaii’s Kīlauea eruption in 2018, when ash carried more than a thousand miles out to sea triggered what may be the largest bloom ever observed in that stretch of the Pacific, an area of ocean roughly five times the size of Taiwan. It happened again around a small Japanese volcanic island in 2020, and around a Mariana Islands volcano in 2003, in one of the most nutrient-poor patches of ocean on the planet.

It’s not just that researchers have watched these processes from space. They’ve collected ash samples from more than a dozen different volcanoes and dunked them in seawater in the lab, measuring precisely how much iron dissolves out of each kind of ash, and how fast. The relationship between iron addition and primary productivity is now understood in minute detail.

Perhaps the most striking recent example doesn’t involve rock at all. During the catastrophic 2019–2020 Australian wildfires, smoke carrying iron-rich ash drifted out over the Southern Ocean, and set off a bloom so large it showed up clearly in satellite images across a huge swath of open water south of Australia — one of the biggest phytoplankton blooms scientists have tracked with modern instruments. It didn’t fade quickly, either: follow-up studies found the bloom kept feeding itself for the better part of nine months, as the ecosystem recycled the iron it had been given rather than using it up and going back to starving.

Smoke from Siberian wildfires has been linked to an amplified plankton bloom near the North Pole. Smoke plumes from Canadian and other boreal fires are the subject of ongoing research into North Atlantic nutrient addition
, with one modeling study projecting that as wildfires become more frequent under a warmer climate, they could boost that ocean’s plant growth by up to a fifth each year. Even California’s 2020 wildfires left a fingerprint on coastal waters near Monterey Bay, shifting which types of phytoplankton were most common. Chemists have also figured out roughly why wildfire ash is such potent fertilizer: the iron in ash from burning is in a form that dissolves more easily in water than the iron locked up in ordinary windblown dust.

A handful of small, remote islands in the Southern Ocean — Kerguelen, Crozet, South Georgia — have been especially intensively studied. They sit in water that would otherwise be as barren as the open ocean around them. Seafloor sediment near these islands leaks iron continuously, and ocean currents smear it out for hundreds of kilometers downstream, producing blooms that come back reliably every single summer. Research teams have spent whole cruises comparing the fertilized water next to these islands with the barren water just beyond it, and the differences are large and consistent: more plankton, more carbon sucked out of the atmosphere and buried in the deep sea, and — in the case of Crozet — measurably more life on the sea floor itself, thousands of meters down, feeding on the extra material raining down from above.

Icebergs do something similar. A landmark 2007 study followed two free-drifting icebergs through the Weddell Sea and found each one trailed a halo of enriched water, extra plankton, extra krill, and extra seabirds for miles around it, as rock dust trapped in the ice thawed out and fed the surrounding sea. Giant icebergs, satellite studies suggest, may supply the iron that feeds the phytoplankton that accounts for as much as a fifth of all the carbon the Southern Ocean buries in a given year.

And then there are the whales. It sounds almost like a joke, but it’s very well established that baleen whale poop carries iron concentrations ten million times higher than ordinary seawater, and Antarctic krill populations collectively hold something like a quarter of all the iron floating in Southern Ocean surface waters within their range. Whales and krill are, in effect, a living fertilizer pump, recycling iron from the deep sea back up to where plankton can use it — and some researchers now suspect that a century of industrial whaling may have quietly throttled Southern Ocean productivity by removing a big part of that pump.

Let’s stand back and evaluate. Even though the mechanisms are wildly different — an underwater volcano is nothing like a dust storm, which is nothing like a burning forest, which is nothing like a whale — they all produce the same basic result: iron-starved water, given iron, grows more phytoplankton, fast and visibly.

This happens on every scale imaginable, from a single ashfall lasting a few days to a chronic, decades-long seep near an island that shows up in the record every single year. And it happens everywhere you look: the North Atlantic, the North Pacific, the Mediterranean, the Arabian Sea, the tropical Pacific, and especially the Southern Ocean, which seems to collect nutrient-addition events from every direction: dust from three continents, ash from island volcanoes, smoke from wildfires, mineral-loaded ice from glaciers, and even whale shit all seem to have broadly similar effects.

So we’re not talking about some wild new speculative hypothesis or an odd-ball observation from a single contested study. We’re talking about the accumulated output of hydrothermal cruises, dust-tracking satellites, floating ocean robots, sediment cores that reach back through ice ages, shipboard chemistry experiments, and decades of chlorophyll imagery. The core relationship — feed iron-starved water, and it grows more phytoplankton, in proportion to how much you feed it, and then stops when the nutrient addition stops — is about as well-established as anything in ocean science gets.

That last point deserves notice. Reversability is a key criterion for ecological safety. You want to be sure that if the intervention you’re pursuing turns out to have a negative effect, you can stop it. You wouldn’t want to risk shifting an ecosystem into a new permanent state. Here the evidence is especially clear. Phytoplankton grows as long as you keep feeding it. When you stop feeding it, it stops growing.

But that’s almost academic, because there is just no evidence of any harm that would need to be reversed. Across dozens of studies on all these sorts of natural experiments, nobody has found any evidence of ecological harm. If you squint, you could say there’s some evidence that in nitrogen-deprived areas, fertilization differentially halps nitrogen-fixing bacteria, but it’s hard for me to see that as a harm: you want to favor the bacteria that fix nitrogen, because they’re the ones that clear the nutrient bottleneck for everything else.

That that’s the closest the literature comes to finding a harm tells you all you need to know. By now, if iron addition was associated with actual some bad outcome —a harmful algal bloom, say, or some kind of toxic nutrient overloading— scientists surely would’ve noticed. But no such thing has been found.

So that gut level sense that we have no idea what might happen if you add iron to iron-starved waters is grounded in ignorance. We have a pretty precise idea what happens when you add nutrients to a marine desert, because we’ve seen it happen again and again all over the world.

In reality, any program to intentionally add nutrients to nutrient-deprived oceans would be considerably less dangerous than any of these natural analogs. Fires, volcanoes, icebergs and vents just dump crap into the ocean willy-nilly, with no plan, no environmental monitoring, no environmental impact assessment, nothing. Nature does this in precisely the kind of blind, reckless, unthinking way that skeptics fear in people, and even then there’s no evidence of harm.

Of course, a program to deliberately increase phytoplankton carbon removal would be infinitely more careful than random geology. It’s hard to figure how intentional nutrient addition carried out under intensive, carefully pre-planned ecological monitoring could be more dangerous than just randomly putting crap in the sea like a volcano does. Thinking it would be is just a sign of anti-human bias.

Which is the point, really.

We have many good reasons to think adding nutrients to the ocean is safe, and very good reasons to believe allowing climate change to run on unchallenged is unsafe. The only thing holding us back is a kind of ideological commitment to the idea that human beings always only get everything wrong, while the random acts of geology are by definition “natural” and therefore good. That’s an ideological argument, not a scientific one.

It had long been hypothesized, but never proven, that shallow undersea vents near Tonga might be leaking iron-rich fluid close enough to the surface to reach sunlit water and feed nitrogen-fixing bacteria.

sentences.en: One case of particular interest to me comes from the subtropical South Pacific.
sentences.zh: 我特别感兴趣的一个案例来自南太平洋亚热带地区。

sentences.en: It had long been hypothesized, but never proven, that shallow undersea vents near Tonga might be leaking iron-rich fluid close enough to the surface to reach sunlit water and feed nitrogen-fixing bacteria.
sentences.zh: 长期以来，人们一直假设，但从未得到证实，汤加附近的浅海海底喷口可能正在泄漏富含铁的液体，距离地表足够近，可以到达阳光照射的水域并喂养固氮细菌。

blocks.html_en: Then, in 2019, a research cruise went and found it.

sentences.en: Then, in 2019, a research cruise went and found it.
sentences.zh: 然后，在 2019 年，一艘研究巡航发现了它。

blocks.html_en: A single string of undersea vents feeds a patch of ocean the size of Germany with enough iron to fuel a bloom of nitrogen-fixing bacteria that grew two to eight times faster than those in the surrounding water, and pulled two to three times more carbon down into the deep ocean. That work was published in Science in 2023. Follow-up cruises mapped exactly how sharply the iron levels spiked near the vents and faded away with distance, tracked the plankton response cell by cell, and even built a nitrogen budget showing that in some seasons, nearly all of the region’s biological productivity runs on vent iron.

sentences.en: A single string of undersea vents feeds a patch of ocean the size of Germany with enough iron to fuel a bloom of nitrogen-fixing bacteria that grew two to eight times faster than those in the surrounding water, and pulled two to three times more carbon down into the deep ocean.
sentences.zh: 一串海底喷口为一片德国大小的海洋提供了足够的铁，为固氮细菌的繁殖提供燃料，这些细菌的生长速度比周围水域的细菌快两到八倍，并向深海吸收了两到三倍的碳。

sentences.en: That work was published in Science in 2023.
sentences.zh: 该成果于 2023 年发表在《科学》杂志上。

sentences.en: Follow-up cruises mapped exactly how sharply the iron levels spiked near the vents and faded away with distance, tracked the plankton response cell by cell, and even built a nitrogen budget showing that in some seasons, nearly all of the region’s biological productivity runs on vent iron.
sentences.zh: 后续巡航准确地绘制了喷口附近铁水平的峰值和随着距离的增加而消失的剧烈程度，逐个细胞地跟踪浮游生物的反应，甚至建立了氮预算，显示在某些季节，该地区几乎所有的生物生产力都依赖于喷口铁。

blocks.html_en: The Tonga-Kermadec range isn’t unique, either. Similar iron-fed blooms have now been traced to volcanic ridges south of Africa, near Antarctica, and scattered across the Pacific seafloor — some fed by iron plumes that drift over a thousand kilometers before they’re used up. One recent study even linked the strength of a Southern Ocean bloom, year by year, to how many small earthquakes had recently shaken the nearby volcanic ridge: more quakes appear to be related to more iron release, which leads to more phytoplankton growth.

sentences.en: The Tonga-Kermadec range isn’t unique, either.
sentences.zh: Tonga-Kermadec 系列也不是独一无二的。

sentences.en: Similar iron-fed blooms have now been traced to volcanic ridges south of Africa, near Antarctica, and scattered across the Pacific seafloor — some fed by iron plumes that drift over a thousand kilometers before they’re used up.
sentences.zh: 现在，类似的以铁为食的水华已被追踪到非洲南部、南极洲附近的火山脊，并散布在太平洋海底——其中一些以铁羽为食，这些铁羽在耗尽之前会漂流一千公里以上。

sentences.en: One recent study even linked the strength of a Southern Ocean bloom, year by year, to how many small earthquakes had recently shaken the nearby volcanic ridge: more quakes appear to be related to more iron release, which leads to more phytoplankton growth.
sentences.zh: 最近的一项研究甚至将南大洋水华的强度逐年与最近震动附近火山脊的小地震数量联系起来：更多的地震似乎与更多的铁释放有关，从而导致更多的浮游植物生长。

blocks.html_en: But in fact, undersea vents are a little niche; the best-known natural fertilizer delivery system is dust in the wind. Every year, wind lifts millions of tons of fine soil off the Sahara and carry it out over the Atlantic; the same thing happens with dust off the Gobi desert blowing over the North Pacific, dust off Patagonia settling onto the Southern Ocean, and dust off drought-stricken southern Africa reaching all the way to Madagascar. Scientists have been measuring these sorts of events for decades: wherever iron-rich dust lands on iron-starved water, phytoplankton growth follows, often within one to two weeks.

sentences.en: But in fact, undersea vents are a little niche; the best-known natural fertilizer delivery system is dust in the wind.
sentences.zh: 但事实上，海底喷口有点小众。最著名的天然肥料输送系统是风中的灰尘。

sentences.en: Every year, wind lifts millions of tons of fine soil off the Sahara and carry it out over the Atlantic; the same thing happens with dust off the Gobi desert blowing over the North Pacific, dust off Patagonia settling onto the Southern Ocean, and dust off drought-stricken southern Africa reaching all the way to Madagascar.
sentences.zh: 每年，风都会将数百万吨的细土带离撒哈拉沙漠，并带到大西洋上空。同样的事情也发生在戈壁沙漠的尘埃吹过北太平洋、巴塔哥尼亚的尘埃落到南大洋、以及遭受干旱的南部非洲的尘埃一直到达马达加斯加。

sentences.en: Scientists have been measuring these sorts of events for decades: wherever iron-rich dust lands on iron-starved water, phytoplankton growth follows, often within one to two weeks.
sentences.zh: 几十年来，科学家们一直在测量此类事件：只要富含铁的尘埃落在缺铁的水域上，浮游植物就会随之生长，通常在一到两周内。

blocks.html_en: Maybe the clearest evidence comes from robotic ocean floats that happened to be sitting in the North Pacific when a Gobi dust cloud rolled over them in 2001. The floats measured the living carbon in the water nearly doubling within two weeks. A dust storm over the Arabian Sea in 2012 was followed by a nearly fivefold jump in chlorophyll. A 2024 study using over a decade of ocean-robot data concluded that nutrients from dust currently sustain roughly a third of all the plant growth happening across the entire Southern Ocean each year — and that during the last ice age, when the world was far dustier, that figure was closer to two-thirds. Deserts, in other words, have been fertilizing oceans on a hemispheric scale for as long as there have been deserts.

sentences.en: Maybe the clearest evidence comes from robotic ocean floats that happened to be sitting in the North Pacific when a Gobi dust cloud rolled over them in 2001.
sentences.zh: 也许最清晰的证据来自2001年戈壁尘云滚过它们时碰巧位于北太平洋的机器人海洋浮标。

sentences.en: The floats measured the living carbon in the water nearly doubling within two weeks.
sentences.zh: 浮标测得水中的活性碳在两周内几乎翻了一番。

sentences.en: A dust storm over the Arabian Sea in 2012 was followed by a nearly fivefold jump in chlorophyll.
sentences.zh: 2012 年阿拉伯海发生沙尘暴后，叶绿素含量猛增近五倍。

sentences.en: A 2024 study using over a decade of ocean-robot data concluded that nutrients from dust currently sustain roughly a third of all the plant growth happening across the entire Southern Ocean each year — and that during the last ice age, when the world was far dustier, that figure was closer to two-thirds.
sentences.zh: 2024 年的一项研究使用了十多年的海洋机器人数据得出结论，目前整个南大洋每年大约三分之一的植物生长来自尘埃的养分，而在上一个冰河时代，当世界尘土飞扬时，这个数字接近三分之二。

sentences.en: Deserts, in other words, have been fertilizing oceans on a hemispheric scale for as long as there have been deserts.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，自从沙漠存在以来，沙漠就一直在半球范围内为海洋施肥。

blocks.html_en: Volcanic ash shows these events in sharp detail, because the nutrient additions are sudden, violent, and easy to date them precisely. In August 2008, an Aleutian volcano called Kasatochi erupted and dropped ash across the Gulf of Alaska. Within days, satellites picked up one of the largest phytoplankton blooms ever recorded in that part of the Pacific. When Iceland’s Eyjafjallajökull volcano — the unpronounceable one that grounded European air traffic back in 2010 — dusted the North Atlantic with ash, research ships sailing under the plume measured a spike in dissolved iron and ran shipboard experiments showing the ash was actively feeding the local plankton. The same thing happened after Hawaii’s Kīlauea eruption in 2018, when ash carried more than a thousand miles out to sea triggered what may be the largest bloom ever observed in that stretch of the Pacific, an area of ocean roughly five times the size of Taiwan. It happened again around a small Japanese volcanic island in 2020, and around a Mariana Islands volcano in 2003, in one of the most nutrient-poor patches of ocean on the planet.

sentences.en: Volcanic ash shows these events in sharp detail, because the nutrient additions are sudden, violent, and easy to date them precisely.
sentences.zh: 火山灰清晰地显示了这些事件的细节，因为营养物质的添加是突然的、猛烈的，而且很容易准确地确定它们的年代。

sentences.en: In August 2008, an Aleutian volcano called Kasatochi erupted and dropped ash across the Gulf of Alaska.
sentences.zh: 2008 年 8 月，一座名为 Kasatochi 的阿留申火山爆发，火山灰飘过阿拉斯加湾。

sentences.en: Within days, satellites picked up one of the largest phytoplankton blooms ever recorded in that part of the Pacific.
sentences.zh: 几天之内，卫星就发现了太平洋该地区有记录以来最大规模的浮游植物大量繁殖之一。

sentences.en: When Iceland’s Eyjafjallajökull volcano — the unpronounceable one that grounded European air traffic back in 2010 — dusted the North Atlantic with ash, research ships sailing under the plume measured a spike in dissolved iron and ran shipboard experiments showing the ash was actively feeding the local plankton.
sentences.zh: 当冰岛的埃亚菲亚德拉冰盖火山——这座难以发音的火山在 2010 年导致欧洲空中交通停飞——给北大西洋带来了火山灰时，在羽流下航行的研究船测量到了溶解铁的峰值，并进行了船上实验，显示火山灰正在积极喂养当地的浮游生物。

sentences.en: The same thing happened after Hawaii’s Kīlauea eruption in 2018, when ash carried more than a thousand miles out to sea triggered what may be the largest bloom ever observed in that stretch of the Pacific, an area of ocean roughly five times the size of Taiwan.
sentences.zh: 2018 年夏威夷基拉韦厄火山喷发后也发生了同样的事情，当时火山灰被带到了一千多英里的海域，引发了太平洋地区有史以来观察到的最大规模的水华，该海域的面积大约是台湾面积的五倍。

sentences.en: It happened again around a small Japanese volcanic island in 2020, and around a Mariana Islands volcano in 2003, in one of the most nutrient-poor patches of ocean on the planet.
sentences.zh: 2020 年，这种情况再次发生在日本的一座小火山岛周围，2003 年，这种情况又发生在马里亚纳群岛的一座火山周围，那里是地球上营养最贫乏的海洋区域之一。

blocks.html_en: It’s not just that researchers have watched these processes from space. They’ve collected ash samples from more than a dozen different volcanoes and dunked them in seawater in the lab, measuring precisely how much iron dissolves out of each kind of ash, and how fast. The relationship between iron addition and primary productivity is now understood in minute detail.

sentences.en: It’s not just that researchers have watched these processes from space.
sentences.zh: 研究人员不仅仅从太空观察了这些过程。

sentences.en: They’ve collected ash samples from more than a dozen different volcanoes and dunked them in seawater in the lab, measuring precisely how much iron dissolves out of each kind of ash, and how fast.
sentences.zh: 他们从十几座不同的火山中收集了火山灰样本，并将它们浸入实验室的海水中，精确测量每种火山灰中铁的溶解量和速度。

sentences.en: The relationship between iron addition and primary productivity is now understood in minute detail.
sentences.zh: 现在，铁添加量与初级生产力之间的关系已经得到了详细的了解。

blocks.html_en: Perhaps the most striking recent example doesn’t involve rock at all. During the catastrophic 2019–2020 Australian wildfires, smoke carrying iron-rich ash drifted out over the Southern Ocean, and set off a bloom so large it showed up clearly in satellite images across a huge swath of open water south of Australia — one of the biggest phytoplankton blooms scientists have tracked with modern instruments. It didn’t fade quickly, either: follow-up studies found the bloom kept feeding itself for the better part of nine months, as the ecosystem recycled the iron it had been given rather than using it up and going back to starving.

sentences.en: Perhaps the most striking recent example doesn’t involve rock at all.
sentences.zh: 也许最近最引人注目的例子根本不涉及摇滚。

sentences.en: During the catastrophic 2019–2020 Australian wildfires, smoke carrying iron-rich ash drifted out over the Southern Ocean, and set off a bloom so large it showed up clearly in satellite images across a huge swath of open water south of Australia — one of the biggest phytoplankton blooms scientists have tracked with modern instruments.
sentences.zh: 在 2019 年至 2020 年澳大利亚灾难性山火期间，携带富含铁的火山灰的烟雾飘过南大洋，引发了巨大的水华，在卫星图像中清晰地显示了澳大利亚南部大片开放水域的水华——这是科学家用现代仪器追踪到的最大的浮游植物水华之一。

sentences.en: It didn’t fade quickly, either: follow-up studies found the bloom kept feeding itself for the better part of nine months, as the ecosystem recycled the iron it had been given rather than using it up and going back to starving.
sentences.zh: 它也没有很快消失：后续研究发现，在九个月的大部分时间里，花朵一直在自我供给，因为生态系统会回收它所获得的铁，而不是用完它并重新陷入饥饿。

blocks.html_en:
Smoke from Siberian wildfires has been linked to an amplified plankton bloom near the North Pole. Smoke plumes from Canadian and other boreal fires are the subject of ongoing research into North Atlantic nutrient addition
, with one modeling study projecting that as wildfires become more frequent under a warmer climate, they could boost that ocean’s plant growth by up to a fifth each year. Even California’s 2020 wildfires left a fingerprint on coastal waters near Monterey Bay, shifting which types of phytoplankton were most common. Chemists have also figured out roughly why wildfire ash is such potent fertilizer: the iron in ash from burning is in a form that dissolves more easily in water than the iron locked up in ordinary windblown dust.

sentences.en: Smoke from Siberian wildfires has been linked to an amplified plankton bloom near the North Pole.
sentences.zh: 西伯利亚野火产生的烟雾与北极附近浮游生物大量繁殖有关。

sentences.en: Smoke plumes from Canadian and other boreal fires are the subject of ongoing research into North Atlantic nutrient addition , with one modeling study projecting that as wildfires become more frequent under a warmer climate, they could boost that ocean’s plant growth by up to a fifth each year.
sentences.zh: 来自加拿大和其他北方火灾的烟羽是北大西洋营养物添加持续研究的主题，一项模型研究预测，随着气候变暖，野火变得更加频繁，它们可以使海洋植物的生长每年增加五分之一。

sentences.en: Even California’s 2020 wildfires left a fingerprint on coastal waters near Monterey Bay, shifting which types of phytoplankton were most common.
sentences.zh: 甚至加州 2020 年的野火也在蒙特利湾附近的沿海水域留下了痕迹，改变了最常见的浮游植物类型。

sentences.en: Chemists have also figured out roughly why wildfire ash is such potent fertilizer: the iron in ash from burning is in a form that dissolves more easily in water than the iron locked up in ordinary windblown dust.
sentences.zh: 化学家还大致弄清楚了为什么野火灰烬是如此有效的肥料：燃烧灰烬中的铁比普通风尘中的铁更容易溶解在水中。

blocks.html_en: A handful of small, remote islands in the Southern Ocean — Kerguelen, Crozet, South Georgia — have been especially intensively studied. They sit in water that would otherwise be as barren as the open ocean around them. Seafloor sediment near these islands leaks iron continuously, and ocean currents smear it out for hundreds of kilometers downstream, producing blooms that come back reliably every single summer. Research teams have spent whole cruises comparing the fertilized water next to these islands with the barren water just beyond it, and the differences are large and consistent: more plankton, more carbon sucked out of the atmosphere and buried in the deep sea, and — in the case of Crozet — measurably more life on the sea floor itself, thousands of meters down, feeding on the extra material raining down from above.

sentences.en: A handful of small, remote islands in the Southern Ocean — Kerguelen, Crozet, South Georgia — have been especially intensively studied.
sentences.zh: 南大洋的一些偏远小岛——凯尔盖朗岛、克罗泽岛、南乔治亚岛——受到了特别深入的研究。

sentences.en: They sit in water that would otherwise be as barren as the open ocean around them.
sentences.zh: 它们坐落在水中，否则它们就会像周围的公海一样贫瘠。

sentences.en: Seafloor sediment near these islands leaks iron continuously, and ocean currents smear it out for hundreds of kilometers downstream, producing blooms that come back reliably every single summer.
sentences.zh: 这些岛屿附近的海底沉积物不断泄漏铁，洋流将其涂抹到下游数百公里处，产生的铁华每年夏天都会可靠地回归。

sentences.en: Research teams have spent whole cruises comparing the fertilized water next to these islands with the barren water just beyond it, and the differences are large and consistent: more plankton, more carbon sucked out of the atmosphere and buried in the deep sea, and — in the case of Crozet — measurably more life on the sea floor itself, thousands of meters down, feeding on the extra material raining down from above.
sentences.zh: 研究小组花了整个航行时间来比较这些岛屿附近的受精水和就在其之外的贫瘠水域，差异是巨大且一致的：更多的浮游生物，更多的碳从大气中吸出并埋在深海中，而且——就克罗泽特而言——海底本身有更多的生物，在数千米深的地方，以从上面落下的额外物质为食。

blocks.html_en: Icebergs do something similar. A landmark 2007 study followed two free-drifting icebergs through the Weddell Sea and found each one trailed a halo of enriched water, extra plankton, extra krill, and extra seabirds for miles around it, as rock dust trapped in the ice thawed out and fed the surrounding sea. Giant icebergs, satellite studies suggest, may supply the iron that feeds the phytoplankton that accounts for as much as a fifth of all the carbon the Southern Ocean buries in a given year.

sentences.en: Icebergs do something similar.
sentences.zh: 冰山也有类似的作用。

sentences.en: A landmark 2007 study followed two free-drifting icebergs through the Weddell Sea and found each one trailed a halo of enriched water, extra plankton, extra krill, and extra seabirds for miles around it, as rock dust trapped in the ice thawed out and fed the surrounding sea.
sentences.zh: 2007年的一项具有里程碑意义的研究跟踪了威德尔海的两座自由漂流的冰山，发现每一座冰山都在其周围数英里的范围内拖着一圈富含水、额外的浮游生物、额外的磷虾和额外的海鸟的光环，因为冰中的岩石灰尘融化并供给了周围的海洋。

sentences.en: Giant icebergs, satellite studies suggest, may supply the iron that feeds the phytoplankton that accounts for as much as a fifth of all the carbon the Southern Ocean buries in a given year.
sentences.zh: 卫星研究表明，巨型冰山可能为浮游植物提供铁质，而浮游植物的碳含量占南大洋当年埋藏碳总量的五分之一。

blocks.html_en: And then there are the whales. It sounds almost like a joke, but it’s very well established that baleen whale poop carries iron concentrations ten million times higher than ordinary seawater, and Antarctic krill populations collectively hold something like a quarter of all the iron floating in Southern Ocean surface waters within their range. Whales and krill are, in effect, a living fertilizer pump, recycling iron from the deep sea back up to where plankton can use it — and some researchers now suspect that a century of industrial whaling may have quietly throttled Southern Ocean productivity by removing a big part of that pump.

sentences.en: And then there are the whales.
sentences.zh: 然后还有鲸鱼。

sentences.en: It sounds almost like a joke, but it’s very well established that baleen whale poop carries iron concentrations ten million times higher than ordinary seawater, and Antarctic krill populations collectively hold something like a quarter of all the iron floating in Southern Ocean surface waters within their range.
sentences.zh: 这听起来几乎像个笑话，但众所周知，须鲸粪便中的铁浓度比普通海水高出一千万倍，而南极磷虾种群在其活动范围内总共含有约四分之一的漂浮在南大洋表层水域的铁。

sentences.en: Whales and krill are, in effect, a living fertilizer pump, recycling iron from the deep sea back up to where plankton can use it — and some researchers now suspect that a century of industrial whaling may have quietly throttled Southern Ocean productivity by removing a big part of that pump.
sentences.zh: 事实上，鲸鱼和磷虾是一个活的肥料泵，将深海中的铁回收到浮游生物可以利用的地方——一些研究人员现在怀疑，一个世纪的工业捕鲸可能已经通过移除该泵的很大一部分而悄悄地扼杀了南大洋的生产力。

blocks.html_en: Let’s stand back and evaluate. Even though the mechanisms are wildly different — an underwater volcano is nothing like a dust storm, which is nothing like a burning forest, which is nothing like a whale — they all produce the same basic result: iron-starved water, given iron, grows more phytoplankton, fast and visibly.

sentences.en: Let’s stand back and evaluate.
sentences.zh: 让我们退后一步来评估一下。

sentences.en: Even though the mechanisms are wildly different — an underwater volcano is nothing like a dust storm, which is nothing like a burning forest, which is nothing like a whale — they all produce the same basic result: iron-starved water, given iron, grows more phytoplankton, fast and visibly.
sentences.zh: 尽管机制截然不同——水下火山与沙尘暴不同，与燃烧的森林不同，与鲸鱼也不同——但它们都会产生相同的基本结果：缺铁的水，如果有铁，会快速而明显地生长更多的浮游植物。

blocks.html_en: This happens on every scale imaginable, from a single ashfall lasting a few days to a chronic, decades-long seep near an island that shows up in the record every single year. And it happens everywhere you look: the North Atlantic, the North Pacific, the Mediterranean, the Arabian Sea, the tropical Pacific, and especially the Southern Ocean, which seems to collect nutrient-addition events from every direction: dust from three continents, ash from island volcanoes, smoke from wildfires, mineral-loaded ice from glaciers, and even whale shit all seem to have broadly similar effects.

sentences.en: This happens on every scale imaginable, from a single ashfall lasting a few days to a chronic, decades-long seep near an island that shows up in the record every single year.
sentences.zh: 这种情况以各种可以想象的规模发生，从持续几天的一次火山灰降落，到每年都会出现在记录中的一座岛屿附近长达数十年的慢性渗漏。

sentences.en: And it happens everywhere you look: the North Atlantic, the North Pacific, the Mediterranean, the Arabian Sea, the tropical Pacific, and especially the Southern Ocean, which seems to collect nutrient-addition events from every direction: dust from three continents, ash from island volcanoes, smoke from wildfires, mineral-loaded ice from glaciers, and even whale shit all seem to have broadly similar effects.
sentences.zh: 这种现象随处可见：北大西洋、北太平洋、地中海、阿拉伯海、热带太平洋，尤其是南大洋，它似乎从各个方向收集营养添加事件：来自三大洲的灰尘、岛屿火山的火山灰、野火产生的烟雾、冰川中富含矿物质的冰，甚至鲸鱼的粪便似乎都具有大致相似的效果。

blocks.html_en: So we’re not talking about some wild new speculative hypothesis or an odd-ball observation from a single contested study. We’re talking about the accumulated output of hydrothermal cruises, dust-tracking satellites, floating ocean robots, sediment cores that reach back through ice ages, shipboard chemistry experiments, and decades of chlorophyll imagery. The core relationship — feed iron-starved water, and it grows more phytoplankton, in proportion to how much you feed it, and then stops when the nutrient addition stops — is about as well-established as anything in ocean science gets.

sentences.en: So we’re not talking about some wild new speculative hypothesis or an odd-ball observation from a single contested study.
sentences.zh: 因此，我们并不是在谈论一些疯狂的新推测假设或来自一项有争议的研究的奇怪观察。

sentences.en: We’re talking about the accumulated output of hydrothermal cruises, dust-tracking satellites, floating ocean robots, sediment cores that reach back through ice ages, shipboard chemistry experiments, and decades of chlorophyll imagery.
sentences.zh: 我们谈论的是热液巡航、尘埃追踪卫星、漂浮海洋机器人、冰河时代的沉积物核心、船上化学实验和数十年的叶绿素图像的累积产出。

sentences.en: The core relationship — feed iron-starved water, and it grows more phytoplankton, in proportion to how much you feed it, and then stops when the nutrient addition stops — is about as well-established as anything in ocean science gets.
sentences.zh: 核心关系——给缺铁的水喂食，它会生长更多的浮游植物，与你喂它的量成正比，然后当营养物质添加停止时停止——这在海洋科学中是最确定的。

blocks.html_en: That last point deserves notice. Reversability is a key criterion for ecological safety. You want to be sure that if the intervention you’re pursuing turns out to have a negative effect, you can stop it. You wouldn’t want to risk shifting an ecosystem into a new permanent state. Here the evidence is especially clear. Phytoplankton grows as long as you keep feeding it. When you stop feeding it, it stops growing.

sentences.en: That last point deserves notice.
sentences.zh: 最后一点值得注意。

sentences.en: Reversability is a key criterion for ecological safety.
sentences.zh: 可逆性是生态安全的关键标准。

sentences.en: You want to be sure that if the intervention you’re pursuing turns out to have a negative effect, you can stop it.
sentences.zh: 您要确保如果您所追求的干预措施产生负面影响，您可以停止它。

sentences.en: You wouldn’t want to risk shifting an ecosystem into a new permanent state.
sentences.zh: 您不想冒险将生态系统转变为新的永久状态。

sentences.en: Here the evidence is especially clear.
sentences.zh: 这里的证据特别清楚。

sentences.en: Phytoplankton grows as long as you keep feeding it.
sentences.zh: 只要你不断喂食浮游植物就会生长。

sentences.en: When you stop feeding it, it stops growing.
sentences.zh: 当你停止喂养它时，它就会停止生长。

blocks.html_en: But that’s almost academic, because there is just no evidence of any harm that would need to be reversed. Across dozens of studies on all these sorts of natural experiments, nobody has found any evidence of ecological harm. If you squint, you could say there’s some evidence that in nitrogen-deprived areas, fertilization differentially halps nitrogen-fixing bacteria, but it’s hard for me to see that as a harm: you want to favor the bacteria that fix nitrogen, because they’re the ones that clear the nutrient bottleneck for everything else.

sentences.en: But that’s almost academic, because there is just no evidence of any harm that would need to be reversed.
sentences.zh: 但这几乎是学术性的，因为没有证据表明有任何伤害需要扭转。

sentences.en: Across dozens of studies on all these sorts of natural experiments, nobody has found any evidence of ecological harm.
sentences.zh: 在所有这些自然实验的数十项研究中，没有人发现任何生态危害的证据。

sentences.en: If you squint, you could say there’s some evidence that in nitrogen-deprived areas, fertilization differentially halps nitrogen-fixing bacteria, but it’s hard for me to see that as a harm: you want to favor the bacteria that fix nitrogen, because they’re the ones that clear the nutrient bottleneck for everything else.
sentences.zh: 如果你眯着眼睛，你可能会说，有一些证据表明，在缺氮地区，施肥会不同程度地抑制固氮细菌，但我很难认为这是一种伤害：你想要支持固氮细菌，因为它们是清除其他一切物质营养瓶颈的细菌。

blocks.html_en: That that’s the closest the literature comes to finding a harm tells you all you need to know. By now, if iron addition was associated with actual some bad outcome —a harmful algal bloom, say, or some kind of toxic nutrient overloading— scientists surely would’ve noticed. But no such thing has been found.

sentences.en: That that’s the closest the literature comes to finding a harm tells you all you need to know.
sentences.zh: 这是文献中最接近发现危害的内容，这告诉了您需要知道的一切。

sentences.en: By now, if iron addition was associated with actual some bad outcome —a harmful algal bloom, say, or some kind of toxic nutrient overloading— scientists surely would’ve noticed.
sentences.zh: 到目前为止，如果铁的添加确实与一些不良后果有关——比如有害的藻华，或者某种有毒的营养物质超载——科学家们肯定会注意到。

sentences.en: But no such thing has been found.
sentences.zh: 但还没有发现这样的事情。

blocks.html_en: So that gut level sense that we have no idea what might happen if you add iron to iron-starved waters is grounded in ignorance. We have a pretty precise idea what happens when you add nutrients to a marine desert, because we’ve seen it happen again and again all over the world.

sentences.en: So that gut level sense that we have no idea what might happen if you add iron to iron-starved waters is grounded in ignorance.
sentences.zh: 因此，我们不知道如果在缺铁的水中添加铁会发生什么，这种直觉是基于无知的。

sentences.en: We have a pretty precise idea what happens when you add nutrients to a marine desert, because we’ve seen it happen again and again all over the world.
sentences.zh: 我们对向海洋沙漠添加营养物质时会发生什么有一个非常精确的了解，因为我们已经看到这种情况在世界各地一次又一次发生。

blocks.html_en: In reality, any program to intentionally add nutrients to nutrient-deprived oceans would be considerably less dangerous than any of these natural analogs. Fires, volcanoes, icebergs and vents just dump crap into the ocean willy-nilly, with no plan, no environmental monitoring, no environmental impact assessment, nothing. Nature does this in precisely the kind of blind, reckless, unthinking way that skeptics fear in people, and even then there’s no evidence of harm.

sentences.en: In reality, any program to intentionally add nutrients to nutrient-deprived oceans would be considerably less dangerous than any of these natural analogs.
sentences.zh: 事实上，任何有意向营养匮乏的海洋添加营养的计划都比任何这些自然类似物的危险要小得多。

sentences.en: Fires, volcanoes, icebergs and vents just dump crap into the ocean willy-nilly, with no plan, no environmental monitoring, no environmental impact assessment, nothing.
sentences.zh: 火灾、火山、冰山和通风口只是随意地将垃圾倒入海洋，没有计划，没有环境监测，没有环境影响评估，什么都没有。

sentences.en: Nature does this in precisely the kind of blind, reckless, unthinking way that skeptics fear in people, and even then there’s no evidence of harm.
sentences.zh: 大自然正是以怀疑论者所担心的那种盲目、鲁莽、不假思索的方式做到了这一点，即便如此，也没有证据表明会造成伤害。

blocks.html_en: Of course, a program to deliberately increase phytoplankton carbon removal would be infinitely more careful than random geology. It’s hard to figure how intentional nutrient addition carried out under intensive, carefully pre-planned ecological monitoring could be more dangerous than just randomly putting crap in the sea like a volcano does. Thinking it would be is just a sign of anti-human bias.

sentences.en: Of course, a program to deliberately increase phytoplankton carbon removal would be infinitely more careful than random geology.
sentences.zh: 当然，故意增加浮游植物碳去除量的计划将比随机地质学更加谨慎。

sentences.en: It’s hard to figure how intentional nutrient addition carried out under intensive, carefully pre-planned ecological monitoring could be more dangerous than just randomly putting crap in the sea like a volcano does.
sentences.zh: 很难想象在密集、精心预先计划的生态监测下有意添加营养物质会比像火山一样随意将垃圾扔进海里更危险。

sentences.en: Thinking it would be is just a sign of anti-human bias.
sentences.zh: 这种想法只是反人类偏见的表现。

blocks.html_en: Which is the point, really.

sentences.en: Which is the point, really.
sentences.zh: 这才是重点，真的。

blocks.html_en: We have many good reasons to think adding nutrients to the ocean is safe, and very good reasons to believe allowing climate change to run on unchallenged is unsafe. The only thing holding us back is a kind of ideological commitment to the idea that human beings always only get everything wrong, while the random acts of geology are by definition “natural” and therefore good. That’s an ideological argument, not a scientific one.

sentences.en: We have many good reasons to think adding nutrients to the ocean is safe, and very good reasons to believe allowing climate change to run on unchallenged is unsafe.
sentences.zh: 我们有很多充分的理由认为向海洋添加营养物质是安全的，也有充分的理由相信让气候变化不受挑战地持续下去是不安全的。

sentences.en: The only thing holding us back is a kind of ideological commitment to the idea that human beings always only get everything wrong, while the random acts of geology are by definition “natural” and therefore good.
sentences.zh: 唯一阻碍我们的是一种意识形态上的承诺，即人类总是只会把一切都弄错，而地质学的随机行为从定义上来说是“自然的”，因此是好的。

sentences.en: That’s an ideological argument, not a scientific one.
sentences.zh: 这是一种意识形态论证，而不是科学论证。

content_html_zh:
向海洋添加营养物质，让微小的海洋植物去除大气中的二氧化碳，这是人们不习惯的想法。 陌生的想法让我们感到不舒服。 “我对此不太确定”的直觉是很自然的。 这是一种不成熟的感觉，这样的事情一定是有风险的，我们以前从未这样做过，所以不可能知道后果会是什么。

但这不是真的。 我们对向营养贫乏的水中添加营养丰富的矿物质时会发生什么有相当精确的了解。 数十次自然实验已经解决了这个问题。 因为大自然一直在通过许多不同的机制将矿物质放入世界各地的海洋中。 数百万年来一直如此。

火山可以做到这一点。 野火可以做到这一点。 即使是受过教育的鲸鱼也会这么做。 沙尘暴和冰山也会造成这种情况。 二十多年来，卫星一直在以微小的精度测量海洋的颜色，而且研究船一直在用传感器和样本瓶追踪这些事件，因此我们有大量且不断增长的记录来显示当营养匮乏的水突然得到补充时会发生什么。 这是海洋科学中记录最齐全的因果关系之一。

请记住，大部分公海都是沙漠。 顶层的水能得到充足的阳光，但随着距离陆地越来越远，构成海洋食物网基础的微小漂流植物——浮游植物——就会缺乏一两种关键成分，通常是铁，有时是氮或磷。 给它们所缺少的东西，它们就会繁殖，并在此过程中吸收空气中的碳。 去掉营养源，几周内效果就会消失。 这种模式在不同的水域、由不同的过程驱动、在任何地方都被反复观察到。

我特别感兴趣的一个案例来自南太平洋亚热带地区。 长期以来，人们一直假设，但从未得到证实，汤加附近的浅海海底喷口可能正在泄漏富含铁的液体，距离地表足够近，可以到达阳光照射的水域并喂养固氮细菌。

然后，在 2019 年，一艘研究巡航发现了它。

一串海底喷口为一片德国大小的海洋提供了足够的铁，为固氮细菌的繁殖提供燃料，这些细菌的生长速度比周围水域的细菌快两到八倍，并向深海吸收了两到三倍的碳。 该成果于 2023 年发表在《科学》杂志上。 后续巡航准确地绘制了喷口附近铁水平的峰值和随着距离的增加而消失的剧烈程度，逐个细胞地跟踪浮游生物的反应，甚至建立了氮预算，显示在某些季节，该地区几乎所有的生物生产力都依赖于喷口铁。

Tonga-Kermadec 系列也不是独一无二的。 现在，类似的以铁为食的水华已被追踪到非洲南部、南极洲附近的火山脊，并散布在太平洋海底——其中一些以铁羽为食，这些铁羽在耗尽之前会漂流一千公里以上。 最近的一项研究甚至将南大洋水华的强度逐年与最近震动附近火山脊的小地震数量联系起来：更多的地震似乎与更多的铁释放有关，从而导致更多的浮游植物生长。

但事实上，海底喷口有点小众。最著名的天然肥料输送系统是风中的灰尘。 每年，风都会将数百万吨的细土带离撒哈拉沙漠，并带到大西洋上空。同样的事情也发生在戈壁沙漠的尘埃吹过北太平洋、巴塔哥尼亚的尘埃落到南大洋、以及遭受干旱的南部非洲的尘埃一直到达马达加斯加。 几十年来，科学家们一直在测量此类事件：只要富含铁的尘埃落在缺铁的水域上，浮游植物就会随之生长，通常在一到两周内。

也许最清晰的证据来自2001年戈壁尘云滚过它们时碰巧位于北太平洋的机器人海洋浮标。 浮标测得水中的活性碳在两周内几乎翻了一番。 2012 年阿拉伯海发生沙尘暴后，叶绿素含量猛增近五倍。 2024 年的一项研究使用了十多年的海洋机器人数据得出结论，目前整个南大洋每年大约三分之一的植物生长来自尘埃的养分，而在上一个冰河时代，当世界尘土飞扬时，这个数字接近三分之二。 换句话说，自从沙漠存在以来，沙漠就一直在半球范围内为海洋施肥。

火山灰清晰地显示了这些事件的细节，因为营养物质的添加是突然的、猛烈的，而且很容易准确地确定它们的年代。 2008 年 8 月，一座名为 Kasatochi 的阿留申火山爆发，火山灰飘过阿拉斯加湾。 几天之内，卫星就发现了太平洋该地区有记录以来最大规模的浮游植物大量繁殖之一。 当冰岛的埃亚菲亚德拉冰盖火山——这座难以发音的火山在 2010 年导致欧洲空中交通停飞——给北大西洋带来了火山灰时，在羽流下航行的研究船测量到了溶解铁的峰值，并进行了船上实验，显示火山灰正在积极喂养当地的浮游生物。 2018 年夏威夷基拉韦厄火山喷发后也发生了同样的事情，当时火山灰被带到了一千多英里的海域，引发了太平洋地区有史以来观察到的最大规模的水华，该海域的面积大约是台湾面积的五倍。 2020 年，这种情况再次发生在日本的一座小火山岛周围，2003 年，这种情况又发生在马里亚纳群岛的一座火山周围，那里是地球上营养最贫乏的海洋区域之一。

研究人员不仅仅从太空观察了这些过程。 他们从十几座不同的火山中收集了火山灰样本，并将它们浸入实验室的海水中，精确测量每种火山灰中铁的溶解量和速度。 现在，铁添加量与初级生产力之间的关系已经得到了详细的了解。

也许最近最引人注目的例子根本不涉及摇滚。 在 2019 年至 2020 年澳大利亚灾难性山火期间，携带富含铁的火山灰的烟雾飘过南大洋，引发了巨大的水华，在卫星图像中清晰地显示了澳大利亚南部大片开放水域的水华——这是科学家用现代仪器追踪到的最大的浮游植物水华之一。 它也没有很快消失：后续研究发现，在九个月的大部分时间里，花朵一直在自我供给，因为生态系统会回收它所获得的铁，而不是用完它并重新陷入饥饿。

西伯利亚野火产生的烟雾与北极附近浮游生物大量繁殖有关。 来自加拿大和其他北方火灾的烟羽是北大西洋营养物添加持续研究的主题，一项模型研究预测，随着气候变暖，野火变得更加频繁，它们可以使海洋植物的生长每年增加五分之一。 甚至加州 2020 年的野火也在蒙特利湾附近的沿海水域留下了痕迹，改变了最常见的浮游植物类型。 化学家还大致弄清楚了为什么野火灰烬是如此有效的肥料：燃烧灰烬中的铁比普通风尘中的铁更容易溶解在水中。

南大洋的一些偏远小岛——凯尔盖朗岛、克罗泽岛、南乔治亚岛——受到了特别深入的研究。 它们坐落在水中，否则它们就会像周围的公海一样贫瘠。 这些岛屿附近的海底沉积物不断泄漏铁，洋流将其涂抹到下游数百公里处，产生的铁华每年夏天都会可靠地回归。 研究小组花了整个航行时间来比较这些岛屿附近的受精水和就在其之外的贫瘠水域，差异是巨大且一致的：更多的浮游生物，更多的碳从大气中吸出并埋在深海中，而且——就克罗泽特而言——海底本身有更多的生物，在数千米深的地方，以从上面落下的额外物质为食。

冰山也有类似的作用。 2007年的一项具有里程碑意义的研究跟踪了威德尔海的两座自由漂流的冰山，发现每一座冰山都在其周围数英里的范围内拖着一圈富含水、额外的浮游生物、额外的磷虾和额外的海鸟的光环，因为冰中的岩石灰尘融化并供给了周围的海洋。 卫星研究表明，巨型冰山可能为浮游植物提供铁质，而浮游植物的碳含量占南大洋当年埋藏碳总量的五分之一。

然后还有鲸鱼。 这听起来几乎像个笑话，但众所周知，须鲸粪便中的铁浓度比普通海水高出一千万倍，而南极磷虾种群在其活动范围内总共含有约四分之一的漂浮在南大洋表层水域的铁。 事实上，鲸鱼和磷虾是一个活的肥料泵，将深海中的铁回收到浮游生物可以利用的地方——一些研究人员现在怀疑，一个世纪的工业捕鲸可能已经通过移除该泵的很大一部分而悄悄地扼杀了南大洋的生产力。

让我们退后一步来评估一下。 尽管机制截然不同——水下火山与沙尘暴不同，与燃烧的森林不同，与鲸鱼也不同——但它们都会产生相同的基本结果：缺铁的水，如果有铁，会快速而明显地生长更多的浮游植物。

这种情况以各种可以想象的规模发生，从持续几天的一次火山灰降落，到每年都会出现在记录中的一座岛屿附近长达数十年的慢性渗漏。 这种现象随处可见：北大西洋、北太平洋、地中海、阿拉伯海、热带太平洋，尤其是南大洋，它似乎从各个方向收集营养添加事件：来自三大洲的灰尘、岛屿火山的火山灰、野火产生的烟雾、冰川中富含矿物质的冰，甚至鲸鱼的粪便似乎都具有大致相似的效果。

因此，我们并不是在谈论一些疯狂的新推测假设或来自一项有争议的研究的奇怪观察。 我们谈论的是热液巡航、尘埃追踪卫星、漂浮海洋机器人、冰河时代的沉积物核心、船上化学实验和数十年的叶绿素图像的累积产出。 核心关系——给缺铁的水喂食，它会生长更多的浮游植物，与你喂它的量成正比，然后当营养物质添加停止时停止——这在海洋科学中是最确定的。

最后一点值得注意。 可逆性是生态安全的关键标准。 您要确保如果您所追求的干预措施产生负面影响，您可以停止它。 您不想冒险将生态系统转变为新的永久状态。 这里的证据特别清楚。 只要你不断喂食浮游植物就会生长。 当你停止喂养它时，它就会停止生长。

但这几乎是学术性的，因为没有证据表明有任何伤害需要扭转。 在所有这些自然实验的数十项研究中，没有人发现任何生态危害的证据。 如果你眯着眼睛，你可能会说，有一些证据表明，在缺氮地区，施肥会不同程度地抑制固氮细菌，但我很难认为这是一种伤害：你想要支持固氮细菌，因为它们是清除其他一切物质营养瓶颈的细菌。

这是文献中最接近发现危害的内容，这告诉了您需要知道的一切。 到目前为止，如果铁的添加确实与一些不良后果有关——比如有害的藻华，或者某种有毒的营养物质超载——科学家们肯定会注意到。 但还没有发现这样的事情。

因此，我们不知道如果在缺铁的水中添加铁会发生什么，这种直觉是基于无知的。 我们对向海洋沙漠添加营养物质时会发生什么有一个非常精确的了解，因为我们已经看到这种情况在世界各地一次又一次发生。

事实上，任何有意向营养匮乏的海洋添加营养的计划都比任何这些自然类似物的危险要小得多。 火灾、火山、冰山和通风口只是随意地将垃圾倒入海洋，没有计划，没有环境监测，没有环境影响评估，什么都没有。 大自然正是以怀疑论者所担心的那种盲目、鲁莽、不假思索的方式做到了这一点，即便如此，也没有证据表明会造成伤害。

当然，故意增加浮游植物碳去除量的计划将比随机地质学更加谨慎。 很难想象在密集、精心预先计划的生态监测下有意添加营养物质会比像火山一样随意将垃圾扔进海里更危险。 这种想法只是反人类偏见的表现。

这才是重点，真的。

我们有很多充分的理由认为向海洋添加营养物质是安全的，也有充分的理由相信让气候变化不受挑战地持续下去是不安全的。 唯一阻碍我们的是一种意识形态上的承诺，即人类总是只会把一切都弄错，而地质学的随机行为从定义上来说是“自然的”，因此是好的。 这是一种意识形态论证，而不是科学论证。
